metadata.seed: You know, I've been asked that question every day for the past two, three weeks. And the only thing I can respond with is what would make a wife cheat on her husband? I mean, it's petty. I know it's petty. But I loved her. And finding out that more than once she was with somebody else, that took something from me that I couldn't get back. And for months I was dealing with. What could I have done? What was it? What did I do? Because it has to have been me. She's too nice a person for that, right? She's too smart a person for that, right? And then weeks of me finally understanding that it wasn't me. A decision that you make is a decision that you make. So when Susie, my administrative assistant, came into the picture, I had a decision that I had to make. I have to take it off and spend time with Susie. Or keep it on and spend many a night alone. So why? You figure it out.

patient.query: You know, I've been asked that question every day for the past two, three weeks. And the only thing I can respond with is what would make a wife cheat on her husband? I mean, it's petty. I know it's petty. But I loved her. And finding out that more than once she was with somebody else, that took something from me that I couldn't get back. And for months I was dealing with. What could I have done? What was it? What did I do? Because it has to have been me. She's too nice a person for that, right? She's too smart a person for that, right? And then weeks of me finally understanding that it wasn't me. A decision that you make is a decision that you make. So when Susie, my administrative assistant, came into the picture, I had a decision that I had to make. I have to take it off and spend time with Susie. Or keep it on and spend many a night alone. So why? You figure it out.

automatic_thoughts: what could I have done?
it has to have been me
she's too nice a person for that
she's too smart a person for that

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly painful to be repeatedly questioning what you might have done wrong, especially after experiencing such a profound betrayal. Can you tell me more about what makes you feel it *has* to be you?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly painful to be repeatedly confronted with this situation and to grapple with such difficult questions about your relationship. It takes a lot of courage to talk about something so deeply hurtful, and it's understandable that you're struggling to make sense of it all.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds incredibly painful to be repeatedly questioning what you might have done wrong, especially after experiencing such a profound betrayal. Can you tell me more about what makes you feel it *has* to be you?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds incredibly painful to be repeatedly questioning yourself and searching for answers when dealing with such a betrayal. What do you think a close friend might say about you blaming yourself for your wife’s choices?

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly painful, and it’s understandable you’re struggling with so many difficult feelings. When you say you've been having thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: Yeah, it really hurts. I just can't shake this feeling that if something goes wrong in a relationship, it must be because of something I did or didn't do. Like, I keep going over our conversations in my head, thinking maybe I said the wrong thing or didn't show enough support. It's exhausting, and I feel like I’m always chasing the ghost of what I could've done differently.

triggers: something goes wrong in a relationship
automatic_thoughts: it must be because of something I did
maybe I said the wrong thing
I didn't show enough support
what I could've done differently

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly painful to be replaying these conversations and feeling responsible for what went wrong. Can you tell me more about what makes you believe it *must* be because of something you did or didn't do?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly painful to replay those conversations and feel responsible for things going wrong in the relationship. That kind of self-blame can be really exhausting.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds incredibly painful to be replaying these conversations and feeling responsible for what went wrong. Can you tell me more about what makes you believe it *must* be because of something you did or didn't do?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds incredibly painful to replay those conversations and feel responsible for what went wrong. Imagine a friend was going through this – what do you think they might say about you blaming yourself so completely?

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly painful, and it's understandable that you're feeling exhausted. When you say you're having these thoughts, are you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: I guess I just feel like if I had been better—more attentive, funnier, more understanding—then things would be different. It’s like I’ve always had this idea that I need to be perfect in relationships, or else they just fall apart. So when they do, I can't help but think that it must be my fault. It makes me feel so alone sometimes, like I’m carrying all of this weight by myself.

automatic_thoughts: if I had been better, things would be different
I need to be perfect in relationships
it must be my fault

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly painful to feel responsible for the outcome of relationships and to carry that weight alone. Can you tell me more about what "being better" looks like in those situations?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly painful to feel responsible for the outcome of relationships and to believe that things would be different if you were "better." That sense of being alone and carrying a heavy weight must be really difficult to manage.

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly painful, carrying that weight and feeling so alone. When you say things would be different if you’d been “better,” were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: Yeah, I think "being better" means being the one who always knows what to say or how to make things right. Like, I feel pressure to read their minds and anticipate their needs. I guess I just want to ensure they feel loved and special all the time, but it feels like I can never quite hit the mark. When I don’t, I worry they might not want to be around me anymore, and that thought is just… terrifying.

automatic_thoughts: I must always know what to say
I need to anticipate their needs
They might not want to be around me

neutral: Character trait finding of emotional warmth (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot of responsibility for other people's feelings – what makes you think "being better" requires you to always know what to say or do? Could there be another way to view this?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like you carry a lot of responsibility for the feelings of others, and it's understandably distressing when you feel like you're not meeting those expectations. That pressure to always "get it right" can be really overwhelming.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot of responsibility for other people's feelings – what makes you think "being better" requires you to always know what to say or do? Could there be another way to view this?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds incredibly stressful to feel responsible for ensuring someone else’s happiness and to worry about their feelings so intensely. What do you think a friend might say about putting so much pressure on yourself to "get it right"?

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly difficult, and it's understandable that you'd feel so much pressure. When you said you worry they might not want to be around you, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: I don’t know... It’s just how I’ve always seen things, I guess. I grew up feeling like I had to take care of everyone around me, almost like it was my job to keep the peace. So now, when I’m in a relationship, it just feels natural to think that if I mess up, it’ll lead to conflict or rejection. I guess it’s hard to imagine that I can just be myself and that might be enough.

automatic_thoughts: I have to take care of everyone
it's my job to keep the peace
if I mess up, it’ll lead to conflict or rejection
I can't just be myself

neutral: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

patient.query: Yeah, it was kind of like I had to be the glue that held everything together. If there was a fight or tension in the house, I’d step in, trying to lighten the mood or solve the problem. I remember feeling like I needed to be the peacemaker all the time, which was exhausting but also made me feel important, I guess? But now, it just feels like that’s the only way I know how to be in a relationship, and sometimes I wonder if I

I have to hold everything together

patient.query: I think I was just really scared of what might happen if I didn’t. Like, if things got too messy or if people got too upset, I didn’t know how to handle that chaos. I just wanted to keep everyone happy and safe, you know? But now, it feels like that instinct just makes me feel even more anxious, like I have to keep proving that I'm worth being around.

patient.query: Yeah, it was a lot of walking on eggshells, really. I remember feeling like I had to be extra careful with my words or actions, especially during family arguments. If I made a joke at the right time or distracted someone, it felt like I could change the mood. But it also felt like this constant pressure to be aware of everyone’s emotions—it’s like if I slipped up, everything would just blow up. I think that’s where this sense of responsibility comes from, and

I can change the mood
